flashcards.back: A complete binary tree where each node follows the heap property: in a max-heap, parent ≥ children; in a min-heap, parent ≤ children.

flashcards.front: Why is a Binary Heap stored as an array?
flashcards.back: It is a complete binary tree with all levels filled (except possibly the last), making array storage space-efficient with O(n) space and O(1) parent/child access.

flashcards.front: Formula for parent index of node at index i (0-indexed)?
Formulas for left and right child of node at index i (0-indexed)?
flashcards.back: Left Child: 2i + 1; Right Child: 2i + 2. For 1-indexed: Left = 2i, Right = 2i + 1.
An O(log n) operation that restores heap property by moving a node down the tree if it violates the heap property with its children.

flashcards.front: What is Sift-up (bubble-up) operation used for?
flashcards.back: Used after inserting a new element at the end of the array. It moves the element up the tree by comparing with parent until heap property is restored. Time: O(log n).

flashcards.front: Steps in Insert operation in Binary Heap
flashcards.back: 1) Add new element at end of array (maintains complete tree property) 2) Apply sift-up to restore heap property. Time: O(log n).
1) Remove root element 2) Move last element to root 3) Apply heapify from root to restore heap property. Time: O(log n).

flashcards.front: What is Peek operation in Binary Heap?
flashcards.back: Returns the maximum (max-heap) or minimum (min-heap) element without removing it. Simply returns arr[0]. Time: O(1).

flashcards.front: How to build a heap from unsorted array?
flashcards.back: Call heapify on all non-leaf nodes starting from floor(n/2)-1 down to 0. Each heapify is O(log n), total O(n). For n elements, there are floor(n/2) non-leaf nodes.
O(n log n) for all cases (best, average, worst) - making it better than quicksort's worst-case O(n²). Space: O(1) if implemented in-place.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Max Heap and Min Heap?
flashcards.back: Max Heap: parent ≥ children (root is maximum). Min Heap: parent ≤ children (root is minimum). Operations are identical, just the comparison operator differs.

flashcards.front: Why is Binary Heap called a Complete Binary Tree?
flashcards.back: All levels are completely filled except possibly the last level, which is filled from left to right. This ensures array representation is contiguous with no gaps.

flashcards.front: How does Binary Heap support Priority Queue efficiently?
flashcards.back: Extract-Max/Min gives O(log n) priority dequeue, Insert gives O(log n) enqueue, Peek gives O(1) access to highest priority. Much better than unsorted array O(n) dequeue.

flashcards.front: What is the height of a Binary Heap with n elements?
flashcards.back: Height = ⌊log₂(n)⌋. For n=7, height=2; for n=8, height=3. This determines worst-case time for heapify operations.
